title: Contains Duplicate
hints: Think about what data structure allows O(1) lookups to check if you've seen an element before.
Iterate through the array and use a set to track seen elements. If you encounter a duplicate, return true.
A set automatically handles uniqueness — if len(set(nums)) < len(nums), there's a duplicate.
approach: Use a hash set to track elements as you iterate. For each element, check if it's already in the set. If yes, return True. Otherwise, add it and continue.
key_insight: A hash set provides O(1) lookup to detect duplicates in a single pass.

title: Valid Anagram
hints: Two strings are anagrams if they have the same character frequencies.
Count character frequencies in both strings and compare them.
You can use a single counter: increment for s, decrement for t, then check all zeros.
approach: Count character frequencies using a hash map (or Counter). Compare the frequency maps of both strings — they must be identical for valid anagrams.
key_insight: Anagrams have identical character frequency distributions. Space is O(1) since the alphabet is fixed (26 letters).

title: Two Sum
hints: For each number, you need to find if its complement (target - num) exists in the array.
Use a hash map to store numbers you've seen and their indices as you iterate.
Check for the complement before adding the current number to avoid using the same element twice.
approach: Iterate through the array while maintaining a hash map of {value: index}. For each element, check if (target - current) exists in the map. If yes, return both indices.
key_insight: Using a hash map to store complements turns a nested loop O(n²) problem into O(n).

title: Group Anagrams
hints: Anagrams share the same set of characters. How can you create a common key for all anagrams?
Sort each string — all anagrams will produce the same sorted string, which can serve as a hash key.
Alternatively, use a tuple of character counts (26 letters) as the key to avoid sorting.
approach: Use a hash map where the key is the sorted version of each string (or a character count tuple). Group strings that share the same key.
key_insight: Sorting each string creates a canonical form that all anagrams share, enabling hash map grouping.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
hints: First, count the frequency of each element using a hash map.
You need the top K by frequency. Think about bucket sort where the index is the frequency.
Create buckets where index i holds all elements with frequency i. Walk from the highest bucket down to collect K elements.
approach: Count frequencies with a hash map, then use bucket sort: create an array of size n+1 where index i contains elements appearing i times. Iterate from the end to collect the top K frequent elements.
key_insight: Bucket sort by frequency achieves O(n) time — better than the O(n log k) heap approach.

title: Encode and Decode Strings
hints: The challenge is choosing a delimiter that won't conflict with string contents.
Prefix each string with its length followed by a special delimiter character.
Use the format 'length#string' for each word. When decoding, read the length, skip the '#', and extract that many characters.
approach: Encode each string as 'length#string'. When decoding, parse the length number, skip the delimiter '#', and read exactly that many characters to reconstruct each original string.
key_insight: Length-prefixing eliminates delimiter ambiguity — the length tells you exactly how many characters to read.

title: Product of Array Except Self
hints: The product of all elements except self equals the product of all elements to the left times the product of all elements to the right.
Compute prefix products (left to right) and suffix products (right to left) for each position.
You can optimize to O(1) extra space by using the output array for prefix products and a running variable for suffix.
approach: First pass: build prefix products from left to right in the output array. Second pass: multiply each position by a running suffix product from right to left.
key_insight: Product except self = prefix product × suffix product. Two passes avoid division entirely.

title: Valid Sudoku
hints: You need to check three constraints: no duplicates in any row, column, or 3×3 box.
Use separate hash sets for each row, column, and box to track seen digits.
Map each cell to its box using (row // 3, col // 3) as the box identifier.
approach: Use hash sets to track digits seen in each row, column, and 3×3 box. Iterate through all cells once, checking and updating the appropriate sets. The box index is determined by (row // 3, col // 3).
key_insight: Map each cell to its 3×3 box with (row // 3, col // 3) and use sets to check all three constraints in one pass.

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
hints: You need O(n) time, so sorting (O(n log n)) is off limits. Think about hash sets.
Put all numbers in a set. A sequence start is a number whose predecessor (num - 1) is NOT in the set.
For each sequence start, count consecutive elements (num+1, num+2, ...) present in the set.
approach: Add all numbers to a hash set. For each number, check if it's the start of a sequence (num-1 not in set). If so, count how far the sequence extends. Track the maximum length.
key_insight: Only start counting from sequence beginnings (where num-1 is absent) to ensure each element is visited at most twice.

title: Valid Palindrome
hints: Use two pointers from both ends, moving inward and comparing characters.
Skip non-alphanumeric characters by advancing the pointer past them.
Compare characters case-insensitively using lower() or toLowerCase().
approach: Use left and right pointers starting from both ends. Skip non-alphanumeric characters. Compare lowercase versions of characters at both pointers. If they ever differ, it's not a palindrome.
key_insight: Two pointers from both ends let you check palindrome properties in-place without creating a new string.

title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
hints: The array is sorted — this means you can use two pointers instead of a hash map.
Start pointers at both ends. If the sum is too large, move the right pointer left; if too small, move left pointer right.
Since the array is sorted, this guarantees you'll find the pair if it exists. Return 1-indexed positions.
approach: Use two pointers at both ends. If sum equals target, return indices (1-indexed). If sum is too small, move left pointer right. If too large, move right pointer left.
key_insight: In a sorted array, two pointers can find a target sum in O(n) time and O(1) space.

title: 3Sum
hints: Sort the array first, then fix one element and use two pointers on the remaining subarray.
Skip duplicate values for the fixed element to avoid duplicate triplets in the result.
After finding a valid triplet, skip duplicates for both left and right pointers too.
approach: Sort the array. For each element nums[i], use two pointers (l=i+1, r=end) to find pairs that sum to -nums[i]. Skip duplicates at all three positions to ensure unique triplets.
key_insight: Reduce 3Sum to Two Sum II by sorting and fixing one element, then using two pointers. Skip duplicates at all levels.

title: Container With Most Water
hints: Start with the widest container (pointers at both ends) and try to find taller lines.
The area is limited by the shorter line. Moving the shorter line inward might find a taller one.
Always move the pointer pointing to the shorter line — moving the taller one can never increase the area.
approach: Use two pointers at both ends. Calculate area = min(height[l], height[r]) * (r - l). Move the pointer at the shorter line inward, since the width is decreasing and only a taller line can compensate.
key_insight: Move the shorter line's pointer inward — the area is bottlenecked by the shorter side, so only changing it can help.

title: Trapping Rain Water
hints: Water at each position = min(max height to the left, max height to the right) - height at that position.
You can precompute leftMax and rightMax arrays, but two pointers achieve O(1) space.
Track leftMax and rightMax with two pointers. Process the side with the smaller max — the water level there is determined.
approach: Use two pointers from both ends with running leftMax and rightMax. Process the side with the smaller max: water trapped = max - height. The smaller max guarantees the water level at that position.
key_insight: Process from the side with the smaller max — water at that position is determined regardless of what's on the other side.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
hints: Track the minimum price seen so far as you iterate through prices.
At each day, calculate the profit if you sold today (price - minPrice).
Update maxProfit if current profit is better, and update minPrice if current price is lower.
approach: Track the minimum price seen so far. At each price, calculate potential profit (current - min). Keep the maximum profit across all days.
key_insight: Track the running minimum buy price — the optimal sell is always at the maximum difference from the lowest point so far.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
hints: Use a sliding window that expands right and contracts left when a duplicate is found.
A hash set tracks characters in the current window for O(1) duplicate checks.
When you find a duplicate, shrink the window from the left until the duplicate is removed.
approach: Maintain a sliding window with a hash set. Expand right by adding characters. When a duplicate is found, shrink from the left (removing characters from the set) until no duplicate exists. Track the maximum window size.
key_insight: Sliding window with a set maintains the longest valid window — each character enters and leaves the set at most once.

title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
hints: A window is valid if (window length - count of most frequent char) <= k.
Use a sliding window that tracks character frequencies. Expand right, shrink left when invalid.
You don't need to update maxFreq when shrinking — an overestimated maxFreq still yields correct results.
approach: Sliding window with character frequency count. A window is valid when (window size - maxFreq) <= k. Expand right, and shrink left when invalid. maxFreq doesn't need to decrease — we only care about finding a longer valid window.
key_insight: Window is valid when (size - maxFreq) ≤ k. maxFreq never needs to decrease since we only care about beating the current best.

title: Permutation in String
hints: A permutation of s1 in s2 means a substring of s2 has the exact same character frequencies as s1.
Use a fixed-size sliding window of length len(s1) over s2.
Track how many character frequencies match between the window and s1. When all 26 match, return true.
approach: Use a fixed-size sliding window of length len(s1). Track character frequency differences and count how many of the 26 characters have matching frequencies. When matches equals 26, a permutation is found.
key_insight: Track the number of matching character frequencies (out of 26). When all match, you've found a permutation.

title: Minimum Window Substring
hints: Use a sliding window that expands to include all required characters, then contracts to find the minimum.
Track how many characters have met their required frequency with a 'formed' counter.
When formed equals the number of unique characters needed, try shrinking from the left.
approach: Expand the window right until all characters in t are covered. Then shrink from the left while maintaining coverage, updating the minimum window. Use frequency maps and a counter for characters that have met their required count.
key_insight: Expand to satisfy the constraint, then contract to minimize — the classic sliding window pattern for 'minimum window' problems.

title: Sliding Window Maximum
hints: A brute force approach checks all k elements for each window. Can you maintain a data structure that gives O(1) max?
Use a monotonic decreasing deque — the front always holds the index of the current maximum.
Remove elements from the back that are smaller than the new element. Remove front elements that fall outside the window.
approach: Use a monotonic decreasing deque storing indices. For each element: remove back elements smaller than current (they'll never be max), remove front if outside window, then the front of deque is the window maximum.
key_insight: A monotonic decreasing deque maintains potential maximums — smaller elements are discarded since they can never be the max while a larger element is in the window.

title: Valid Parentheses
hints: Use a stack to match opening brackets with their closing counterparts.
Push opening brackets onto the stack. When you see a closing bracket, check if the top of the stack matches.
If the stack is empty when you see a closing bracket, or mismatched, return false. At the end, the stack must be empty.
approach: Push opening brackets onto a stack. For each closing bracket, check if the stack top is the matching opener. Return true only if the stack is empty at the end.
key_insight: A stack naturally handles nested structure — the most recent unmatched opener should match the next closer.

title: Min Stack
hints: You need O(1) getMin. How can you track the minimum as elements are pushed and popped?
Store the current minimum alongside each element in the stack.
Each stack entry is a pair (value, current_min). When pushing, the new min is min(val, current top's min).
approach: Each stack entry stores both the value and the minimum value at that point. When pushing, compute the new minimum as min(val, current min). This way getMin is always O(1) by reading the top entry's min.
key_insight: Store (value, current_min) pairs so the minimum is always accessible at the top of the stack.

title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
hints: RPN evaluation is a classic stack problem — operands are pushed, operators pop two and push the result.
When you see a number, push it. When you see an operator, pop two numbers, apply the operation, push the result.
Be careful with division: it should truncate toward zero (use int() in Python, Math.trunc() in JS).
approach: Iterate through tokens. Push numbers onto the stack. When encountering an operator, pop two operands, apply the operation (with truncation toward zero for division), and push the result. The final stack element is the answer.
key_insight: A stack naturally evaluates postfix expressions — operators always apply to the two most recent operands.

title: Generate Parentheses
hints: Use backtracking to generate valid combinations. At each step, decide to add '(' or ')'.
You can add '(' if open count < n. You can add ')' if close count < open count.
When the combination length reaches 2*n, it's a complete valid combination.
approach: Backtrack by choosing to add '(' (if open < n) or ')' (if close < open) at each step. When the string length reaches 2n, add it to results. This ensures only valid combinations are generated.
key_insight: Only add ')' when close < open — this single rule guarantees all generated combinations are valid.

title: Daily Temperatures
hints: For each day, you need the next warmer day. This is a 'next greater element' problem.
Use a monotonic decreasing stack that stores indices of unresolved days.
When the current temperature is warmer than the stack top, pop and compute the difference in days.
approach: Use a monotonic decreasing stack of indices. For each day, while the current temperature exceeds the stack top's temperature, pop and set result[popped] = current_index - popped_index. Push current index.
key_insight: A monotonic decreasing stack efficiently finds the next greater element — each element is pushed and popped at most once.

title: Car Fleet
hints: Calculate how long each car takes to reach the target. Cars closer to the target that are slower will block faster cars behind them.
Sort cars by position in descending order (closest to target first).
Use a stack: if the current car's time is greater than the top, it forms a new fleet. Otherwise, it merges into the fleet ahead.
approach: Calculate time to target for each car. Sort by position descending. Use a stack: push times. If current time <= stack top, it catches up (merges). If greater, it's a new fleet. Stack size = number of fleets.
key_insight: Sort by position descending and use a stack of arrival times — a slower car ahead forces faster cars behind to merge into its fleet.

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
hints: For each bar, the largest rectangle using that bar as the shortest extends left and right to bars that are at least as tall.
A monotonic increasing stack tracks bars. When a shorter bar is encountered, pop taller bars and calculate their rectangles.
The width of each popped bar's rectangle extends from the new stack top to the current index.
approach: Maintain a monotonic increasing stack of (index, height). When a bar shorter than the stack top is found, pop and calculate area using the popped height and the width from the current stack top to the current index. Push remaining bars at the end.
key_insight: A monotonic increasing stack lets you determine, for each bar, how far left and right it can extend as the minimum height.

title: Binary Search
hints: Classic binary search: compare the middle element with the target to halve the search space.
If target < mid, search left half. If target > mid, search right half.
Use l <= r as the loop condition and return -1 if the loop exits without finding the target.
approach: Standard binary search on a sorted array. Maintain left and right pointers. Compare target with the middle element and narrow the search space accordingly.
key_insight: Halving the search space each iteration gives O(log n) — the foundation of all binary search variants.

title: Search a 2D Matrix
hints: The matrix rows are sorted and each row starts after the previous row ends — it's essentially one sorted array.
Treat the 2D matrix as a 1D sorted array with index mapping: row = idx // cols, col = idx % cols.
Apply standard binary search on indices 0 to (rows * cols - 1).
approach: Treat the matrix as a flattened sorted array. Use binary search on indices 0 to m*n-1, converting each index to (row, col) with row = idx // n, col = idx % n.
key_insight: Flatten the 2D matrix conceptually into a 1D sorted array using index math: row = idx // cols, col = idx % cols.

title: Koko Eating Bananas
hints: Binary search on the answer: the eating speed k. The range is 1 to max(piles).
For a given speed k, calculate total hours needed: sum of ceil(pile / k) for each pile.
If total hours <= h, k might work (try slower). If > h, k is too slow (try faster).
approach: Binary search on the eating speed k (1 to max(piles)). For each k, compute hours needed. If hours <= h, try a smaller k. Otherwise, try larger k. Return the minimum valid k.
key_insight: Binary search on the answer — when the search space is a range of valid answers, binary search finds the optimal one.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
hints: The array is sorted but rotated. The minimum is at the rotation point.
Compare mid with the rightmost element. If mid > right, the min is in the right half.
If mid <= right, the min is in the left half (including mid).
approach: Binary search comparing mid with the right boundary. If nums[mid] > nums[r], the minimum is in the right half (l = mid + 1). Otherwise, it's in the left half including mid (r = mid).
key_insight: Compare mid with the right boundary — if mid > right, the rotation point (minimum) must be to the right of mid.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
hints: One half of the array is always sorted. Determine which half is sorted and check if the target lies in it.
If nums[l] <= nums[mid], the left half is sorted. Check if target is in [l, mid).
Otherwise, the right half is sorted. Check if target is in (mid, r].
approach: At each step, one half is sorted. Determine which half, then check if the target falls within that sorted half. If yes, search there. If no, search the other half.
key_insight: In a rotated sorted array, one half is always sorted — determine which half and whether the target falls within it.

title: Time Based Key-Value Store
hints: Store values with their timestamps in a list for each key. Timestamps are always increasing.
For get(), binary search the list to find the largest timestamp <= the given timestamp.
Use a hash map of key -> sorted list of (timestamp, value) pairs.
approach: Use a hash map where each key maps to a list of (timestamp, value) pairs (naturally sorted since set is called with increasing timestamps). For get, binary search to find the largest timestamp ≤ the query timestamp.
time_complexity: O(log n) per get, O(1) per set
key_insight: Timestamps arrive in order so the list is naturally sorted — binary search gives O(log n) lookups for the floor timestamp.

title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
hints: Binary search on the partition of the smaller array. The total left partition should have (m+n+1)/2 elements.
For a valid partition, maxLeft1 <= minRight2 and maxLeft2 <= minRight1.
If maxLeft1 > minRight2, move the partition left in array 1. Otherwise, move it right.
approach: Binary search on the smaller array to find a partition where the left halves of both arrays contain exactly half the total elements, and all left elements ≤ all right elements. The median is derived from the boundary elements.
key_insight: Binary search on the partition point of the smaller array — a correct partition means all left elements ≤ all right elements.

title: Reverse Linked List
hints: You need to reverse the direction of all pointers in the list.
Use three pointers: prev, curr, and next. At each step, point curr.next to prev.
Save curr.next before overwriting it, then advance prev and curr.
approach: Iterate through the list with prev and curr pointers. At each node, save next, reverse the pointer (curr.next = prev), then advance both pointers. Return prev as the new head.
key_insight: Three pointers (prev, curr, next) let you reverse each link in one pass without extra space.

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
hints: Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases when building the merged list.
Compare the heads of both lists, append the smaller one to the result, and advance that pointer.
When one list is exhausted, append the remainder of the other list.
approach: Create a dummy head. Compare nodes from both lists, always appending the smaller one. When one list runs out, append the rest of the other. Return dummy.next.
key_insight: A dummy head node eliminates special cases for the first element and simplifies list construction.

title: Reorder List
hints: The reordered list interleaves from the start and end. Think about how to access the end efficiently.
Split the list in half (use slow/fast pointers), reverse the second half, then merge alternately.
Three steps: find middle, reverse second half, merge the two halves.
approach: 1) Find the middle using slow/fast pointers. 2) Reverse the second half. 3) Merge the two halves by alternating nodes from each.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def reorderList(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> None:
        slow, fast = head, head.next
        while fast and fast.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
        second = slow.next
        slow.next = None
        prev = None
        while second:
            nxt = second.next
            second.next = prev
            prev = second
            second = nxt
        first, second = head, prev
        while second:
            tmp1, tmp2 = first.next, second.next
            first.next = second
            second.next = tmp1
            first, second = tmp1, tmp2
key_insight: Decompose into three classic operations: find middle (slow/fast), reverse list, and merge two lists.

title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
hints: To find the nth node from the end in one pass, use two pointers spaced n nodes apart.
Advance the fast pointer n steps ahead, then move both until fast reaches the end.
Use a dummy node before head to handle the case where the head itself needs to be removed.
approach: Use a dummy node and two pointers. Advance fast n+1 steps ahead. Move both pointers until fast is null. Now slow is right before the node to remove. Skip it with slow.next = slow.next.next.
key_insight: Two pointers spaced n apart — when fast hits the end, slow is at the node before the one to remove.

title: Copy List with Random Pointer
hints: The challenge is mapping original nodes to their copies so random pointers can be set correctly.
Use a hash map: key = original node, value = copied node.
Two passes: first create all copied nodes (building the map), then set next and random pointers.
approach: Use a hash map mapping original nodes to copies. First pass: create copy nodes. Second pass: set next and random pointers using the map. Alternatively, use a defaultdict that lazily creates copies.
key_insight: A hash map from original to copy nodes lets you resolve random pointers in a second pass.

title: Add Two Numbers
hints: Numbers are stored in reverse order, so the head is the least significant digit — just like manual addition.
Add corresponding digits plus carry. The new digit is sum % 10, the new carry is sum // 10.
Don't forget the final carry — if it's non-zero, create one more node.
approach: Traverse both lists simultaneously, adding corresponding digits plus carry. Create new nodes with (sum % 10) and track carry (sum // 10). Continue until both lists are exhausted and carry is 0.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def addTwoNumbers(self, l1: Optional[ListNode], l2: Optional[ListNode]) -> Optional[ListNode]:
        dummy = ListNode()
        curr = dummy
        carry = 0
        while l1 or l2 or carry:
            v1 = l1.val if l1 else 0
            v2 = l2.val if l2 else 0
            total = v1 + v2 + carry
            carry = total // 10
            curr.next = ListNode(total % 10)
            curr = curr.next
            l1 = l1.next if l1 else None
            l2 = l2.next if l2 else None
        return dummy.next
key_insight: Reverse-order storage means digits align perfectly for addition — just simulate grade-school addition with carry.

title: Linked List Cycle
hints: If there's a cycle, a fast pointer (2 steps) will eventually meet a slow pointer (1 step).
This is Floyd's cycle detection algorithm (tortoise and hare).
If fast reaches null, there's no cycle. If fast equals slow, there is one.
approach: Floyd's cycle detection: move slow pointer 1 step and fast pointer 2 steps. If they meet, there's a cycle. If fast reaches null, there's no cycle.
key_insight: Floyd's tortoise and hare — a fast pointer lapping a slow pointer proves a cycle exists, using only O(1) space.

title: Find the Duplicate Number
hints: Treat the array as a linked list where nums[i] points to index nums[i]. The duplicate creates a cycle.
Use Floyd's cycle detection to find where slow and fast meet inside the cycle.
Then reset one pointer to start. Move both at speed 1 — they meet at the cycle entrance (the duplicate).
approach: Model the array as a linked list (index -> value). A duplicate means two indices point to the same value, creating a cycle. Use Floyd's algorithm: find the meeting point, then find the cycle entrance.
key_insight: Reinterpret the array as a linked list — the duplicate number is the entrance to the cycle, found via Floyd's algorithm.

title: LRU Cache
hints: You need O(1) get and put. A hash map gives O(1) lookup, but how do you track usage order?
Use a doubly linked list to maintain usage order. Most recently used at the head, least at the tail.
Combine a hash map (key -> node) with a doubly linked list. On access, move the node to the head.
approach: Combine a hash map (key -> node) with a doubly linked list (ordered by recency). Get: look up in map, move to front. Put: insert at front, evict from tail if capacity exceeded. Both O(1).
key_insight: Hash map + doubly linked list gives O(1) for both lookup and order manipulation. JS Map maintains insertion order natively.

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
hints: A min-heap of size k can always give you the smallest element across all lists.
Push the head of each list into the heap. Pop the smallest, add it to the result, and push its next node.
Alternatively, use divide and conquer: merge pairs of lists recursively like merge sort.
approach: Use a min-heap to always extract the smallest node across all k lists. Push heads initially, then after popping a node, push its next node. Build the result list from popped nodes.
key_insight: A min-heap of k elements efficiently selects the global minimum. Divide-and-conquer merge is an alternative O(n log k) approach.

title: Reverse Nodes in k-Group
hints: First, check if there are at least k nodes remaining. If not, leave them as-is.
Reverse k nodes using the standard iterative reversal technique.
After reversing a group, connect it to the previous group's tail and recurse/iterate for the next group.
approach: For each group: 1) Check if k nodes exist. 2) Reverse k nodes. 3) Connect the reversed group to previous and next groups. Use a dummy node and track the group's predecessor for reconnection.
key_insight: Reverse each k-group independently, using a predecessor pointer to reconnect reversed segments to the rest of the list.

title: Invert Binary Tree
hints: Inverting a tree means swapping every node's left and right children.
Use recursion: swap the children, then recursively invert each subtree.
Base case: if the node is null, return null.
approach: Recursively swap left and right children at every node. The base case is a null node. After swapping, recurse on both children.
key_insight: Swap children at every node — the recursive structure naturally handles the entire tree.

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
hints: The depth of a tree = 1 + max(depth of left subtree, depth of right subtree).
Use recursion with the base case: an empty node has depth 0.
Each recursive call adds 1 to the depth of its deeper child.
approach: Recursive DFS: the depth of a node is 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)). Base case: null node returns 0.
key_insight: Tree depth is a natural recursive problem — the depth of any node depends only on the depths of its children.

title: Diameter of Binary Tree
hints: The diameter passes through some node where it equals left_height + right_height.
At each node, compute the height and update a global max diameter.
The height function returns the depth, while the diameter is tracked as a side effect.
approach: DFS returning height at each node. The diameter through any node = left_height + right_height. Track the maximum diameter seen across all nodes as a side effect of the height computation.
key_insight: Piggyback on the height computation — at each node, left_height + right_height gives the diameter through that node.

title: Balanced Binary Tree
hints: A balanced tree has subtrees where the height difference is at most 1 at every node.
Compute height recursively but return -1 if any subtree is unbalanced.
If either child returns -1, propagate -1 upward. Otherwise check |left - right| <= 1.
approach: DFS returning height, but return -1 to signal imbalance. At each node, if either subtree is -1 or heights differ by more than 1, return -1. Otherwise return 1 + max(left, right).
key_insight: Use -1 as a sentinel for 'unbalanced' — it short-circuits the height computation and avoids redundant work.

title: Same Tree
hints: Two trees are the same if their root values match and their left and right subtrees are the same.
Base cases: both null = same; one null and one not = different.
Recursively compare: values match AND left subtrees match AND right subtrees match.
approach: Recursive comparison: both null → true, one null → false, values differ → false. Otherwise recursively check both subtrees.
key_insight: Structural equality of trees reduces to: same value at root, and recursive equality of both subtrees.

title: Subtree of Another Tree
hints: For each node in the main tree, check if the subtree rooted there is identical to the target tree.
Reuse the 'same tree' check at each node of the main tree.
Recursively traverse the main tree, calling isSameTree at each node.
approach: At each node in the main tree, check if the subtree starting there matches the target using isSameTree. If not, recursively check left and right children.
key_insight: Subtree check = traverse the main tree and run isSameTree at each node. It composes two simple recursive functions.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
hints: In a BST, if both nodes are smaller than root, the LCA is in the left subtree.
If both are larger, the LCA is in the right subtree.
If they split (one on each side, or one equals root), the current node is the LCA.
approach: Leverage BST property: if both p and q are less than root, go left. If both greater, go right. Otherwise the current node is the LCA (the split point).
key_insight: The LCA in a BST is the first node where p and q diverge to different subtrees (or equal the node).

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
hints: BFS naturally processes nodes level by level using a queue.
At each level, process all nodes currently in the queue (track queue size at the start of each level).
Collect values for each level into a sub-list before moving to the next level.
approach: BFS with a queue. At each level, record the queue size, process that many nodes (adding their children), and collect their values into a level list.
key_insight: Process nodes in batches of queue.size() to separate levels — this is the core BFS level-order pattern.

title: Binary Tree Right Side View
hints: The right side view is the last node at each level when doing level-order traversal.
Use BFS and at each level, record the last node's value.
Alternatively, use DFS visiting right child first, and add the first node seen at each depth.
approach: BFS level-order traversal. At each level, the last node processed is the rightmost visible node. Add it to the result.
key_insight: The right side view is simply the last node at each BFS level — a small modification to level-order traversal.

title: Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree
hints: A node is 'good' if no node on the path from root to it has a greater value.
Pass the maximum value seen so far as a parameter during DFS.
If the current node's value >= max so far, it's a good node. Update the max and continue.
approach: DFS passing the maximum value seen on the path from root. A node is good if its value >= the path maximum. Update the max and recurse on both children, summing good nodes.
key_insight: Pass the path maximum as a DFS parameter — a node is 'good' if it meets or exceeds all ancestors on its path.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
hints: Each node must fall within a valid range determined by its ancestors.
Pass lower and upper bounds down the recursion. The root has (-inf, inf).
Left child gets (lower, parent.val) as bounds; right child gets (parent.val, upper).
approach: DFS with min/max bounds. Each node must be within (low, high). Left child's upper bound is the parent's value; right child's lower bound is the parent's value.
key_insight: Pass valid range bounds (low, high) down the recursion — every node must satisfy low < val < high.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
hints: In-order traversal of a BST visits nodes in sorted ascending order.
Perform in-order traversal and count nodes visited. The kth node visited is the answer.
Use iterative in-order with a stack to avoid processing all nodes when k is small.
approach: Iterative in-order traversal using a stack. Decrement k each time a node is popped. When k reaches 0, that node's value is the answer.
key_insight: In-order traversal of a BST yields sorted order — the kth node visited is the kth smallest element.

title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
hints: The first element of preorder is always the root. Find it in inorder to determine left/right subtree sizes.
Elements left of the root in inorder belong to the left subtree; elements right belong to the right subtree.
Use a hash map for O(1) lookups of positions in inorder. Recursively build left and right subtrees.
approach: Preorder's first element is the root. Find it in inorder (using a hash map) to split into left/right subtrees. Recursively build each subtree using the appropriate slices of preorder and inorder.
key_insight: Preorder gives the root; inorder splits the tree into left and right subtrees. A hash map makes root lookups O(1).

title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
hints: A path can start and end at any nodes. At each node, the path can go through it connecting left and right subtrees.
At each node, compute the max single-path sum (node + best child). Update the global max with (left + node + right).
If a subtree contributes a negative sum, treat it as 0 (don't include it).
approach: DFS returning the max single-branch path from each node. At each node, calculate the path through it (left + node + right) and update the global maximum. Return node + max(left, right) for the parent to use.
key_insight: At each node, the best path through it uses both children, but the value returned to the parent can only use one branch.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
hints: Use preorder traversal for serialization, marking null nodes with a sentinel like 'N'.
Serialize: visit root, then left, then right. Use a delimiter between values.
Deserialize: consume values from the serialized list in preorder. 'N' means create a null node.
approach: Serialize via preorder DFS, recording 'N' for null nodes. Deserialize by consuming the serialized values in order: create a node, recursively build left then right subtrees.
key_insight: Preorder traversal with null markers uniquely defines a binary tree — the deserialization just replays the traversal.

title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
hints: A trie node has children (one per character) and a flag indicating if a word ends here.
Insert: walk down the trie, creating nodes for missing characters. Mark the last node as end-of-word.
Search: walk down the trie. Return true only if all characters exist AND the last node is marked as end-of-word.
approach: Each trie node has a children dict and an end-of-word flag. Insert adds nodes for each character. Search traverses and checks the flag. StartsWith traverses without checking the flag.
key_insight: A trie stores words character-by-character in a tree — shared prefixes share nodes, making prefix operations efficient.

title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
hints: The '.' wildcard matches any single character — this requires exploring multiple branches.
Use a trie for storage. Add works like normal trie insert.
For search, when you encounter '.', recursively try all children at that position.
approach: Build a trie. AddWord is standard insertion. Search uses DFS: for normal characters, follow the child. For '.', try all children recursively. Return true if any path reaches a valid end.
time_complexity: O(m) for add, O(26^m) worst case for search with dots
key_insight: The '.' wildcard transforms trie search into a DFS that branches at each wildcard position, exploring all possible matches.

title: Word Search II
hints: Build a trie from all words, then DFS from each cell in the board following the trie.
The trie lets you prune DFS branches early — if no word has this prefix, stop exploring.
Mark found words and prune trie branches to avoid duplicate results and speed up search.
approach: Build a trie from all words. For each cell, DFS following trie children. When a word-end is reached, add it to results. Prune trie branches after finding words. Mark visited cells to avoid revisiting.
key_insight: A trie built from all words guides the DFS — it simultaneously searches for all words and prunes impossible paths.

title: Kth Largest Element in a Stream
hints: Think about which data structure lets you efficiently track the k largest elements.
A min-heap of size k keeps the kth largest element at the top.
When the heap exceeds size k, pop the smallest element to maintain only the k largest.
approach: Maintain a min-heap of size k. For each new element, push it onto the heap. If the heap size exceeds k, pop the minimum. The top of the heap is always the kth largest element.
time_complexity: O(n log k) init, O(log k) add
key_insight: A min-heap of size k naturally keeps the kth largest at the top, since all elements below the kth largest get removed.

title: Last Stone Weight
hints: You need to repeatedly find the two heaviest stones efficiently.
A max-heap lets you extract the two largest elements in O(log n) time.
In Python, negate values to simulate a max-heap with heapq.
approach: Use a max-heap (negate values in Python). Repeatedly pop the two largest stones. If they differ, push the difference back. Continue until one or zero stones remain.
key_insight: A max-heap gives O(log n) access to the largest element, perfect for simulating the stone-smashing process.

title: K Closest Points to Origin
hints: You need the k smallest distances — think about which heap type helps find top-k elements.
A max-heap of size k lets you track the k closest points: if a new point is closer than the farthest in the heap, swap them.
You don't need to take the square root — comparing squared distances preserves ordering.
approach: Use a max-heap of size k. For each point, compute the squared distance and push it. If the heap exceeds size k, pop the largest. The remaining k elements are the k closest points.
key_insight: For top-k smallest problems, use a max-heap of size k — it efficiently discards elements larger than the kth smallest.

title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
hints: A brute force approach sorts the array, but can you do better?
A min-heap of size k keeps the kth largest at the top.
Alternatively, Quickselect gives O(n) average time by partitioning around a pivot.
approach: Use a min-heap of size k. Push each element; if size exceeds k, pop the minimum. The heap top is the kth largest. Alternatively, use Quickselect for O(n) average time.
key_insight: A min-heap of size k naturally maintains the kth largest element at the top — all smaller elements have been evicted.

title: Task Scheduler
hints: The most frequent task determines the minimum number of intervals needed.
Use a max-heap to always schedule the most frequent remaining task.
Use a cooldown queue to track when tasks become available again.
approach: Count task frequencies. Use a max-heap to greedily pick the most frequent available task each interval. After executing a task, place it in a cooldown queue with its next available time. Process until both heap and queue are empty.
time_complexity: O(n * m) where m is number of tasks
key_insight: Greedily scheduling the most frequent task minimizes idle time; the cooldown queue ensures tasks respect the gap constraint.

title: Design Twitter
hints: How do you merge multiple sorted lists of tweets into one feed?
Use a max-heap to merge the most recent tweets from all followed users.
Maintain a global timestamp to order tweets chronologically.
approach: Track followers with a hash map of sets. Store tweets per user with timestamps. For getNewsFeed, use a max-heap to merge-sort tweets from all followed users (including self), returning the 10 most recent.
time_complexity: O(k log n) for getNewsFeed where k=10, n=followers
space_complexity: O(N) for all tweets and follow relationships
key_insight: Getting the news feed is a k-way merge problem — use a heap to efficiently merge sorted tweet lists from followed users.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
hints: The median divides a sorted list into two halves — think about maintaining both halves.
Use a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half.
Keep the heaps balanced (sizes differ by at most 1) and the median is at the top of one or both heaps.
approach: Maintain two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half. When adding a number, insert into the appropriate heap and rebalance so sizes differ by at most 1. The median is the top of the larger heap, or the average of both tops.
key_insight: Two heaps split the data into lower and upper halves, giving O(1) access to the middle element(s) for median calculation.

title: Subsets
hints: Each element can either be included or excluded from a subset.
Use backtracking: at each index, branch into include/exclude decisions.
The base case is when you've considered all elements — add the current subset to results.
approach: Use backtracking. At each index, make two recursive calls: one including the current element and one excluding it. When the index reaches the end of the array, add the current subset to the result.
solution_python:
def subsets(self, nums: List[int]) -> List[List[int]]:
    res = []
    subset = []
    def backtrack(i):
        if i >= len(nums):
            res.append(subset[:])
            return
        subset.append(nums[i])
        backtrack(i + 1)
        subset.pop()
        backtrack(i + 1)
    backtrack(0)
    return res
key_insight: Each element has a binary choice (include/exclude), creating a decision tree with 2^n leaves — one for each subset.

title: Combination Sum
hints: Each candidate can be used unlimited times — how does this change your recursion?
When you include a candidate, don't advance the index — recurse with the same index to allow reuse.
When you skip a candidate, advance the index to avoid duplicate combinations.
approach: Backtrack with an index. At each step, either include the current candidate (without advancing index, allowing reuse) or skip it (advance index). Stop when the target reaches 0 (found solution) or goes negative (prune).
solution_python:
def combinationSum(self, candidates: List[int], target: int) -> List[List[int]]:
    res = []
    def backtrack(i, cur, total):
        if total == target:
            res.append(cur[:])
            return
        if i >= len(candidates) or total > target:
            return
        cur.append(candidates[i])
        backtrack(i, cur, total + candidates[i])
        cur.pop()
        backtrack(i + 1, cur, total)
    backtrack(0, [], 0)
    return res
key_insight: Allowing reuse means we recurse with the same index when including an element, but advance when excluding to prevent duplicates.

title: Permutations
hints: Unlike subsets, permutations use all elements — order matters.
At each position, try every element that hasn't been used yet.
Track used elements with a set or by swapping elements in-place.
approach: Backtrack by building the permutation one element at a time. At each step, try each unused element, add it to the current permutation, recurse, then backtrack. When the permutation length equals the input length, record it.
key_insight: Permutations require trying every unused element at each position — the decision tree has n choices, then n-1, then n-2, yielding n! leaves.

title: Subsets II
hints: This is like Subsets, but with duplicates — how do you avoid duplicate subsets?
Sort the array first so duplicates are adjacent.
When skipping an element, skip all consecutive duplicates to avoid generating the same subset.
approach: Sort the input. Use backtracking similar to Subsets, but when excluding an element, skip over all duplicate values to prevent duplicate subsets in the result.
solution_python:
def subsetsWithDup(self, nums: List[int]) -> List[List[int]]:
    res = []
    nums.sort()
    def backtrack(i, subset):
        if i >= len(nums):
            res.append(subset[:])
            return
        subset.append(nums[i])
        backtrack(i + 1, subset)
        subset.pop()
        while i + 1 < len(nums) and nums[i] == nums[i + 1]:
            i += 1
        backtrack(i + 1, subset)
    backtrack(0, [])
    return res
key_insight: Sorting + skipping consecutive duplicates on the 'exclude' branch prevents generating duplicate subsets.

title: Combination Sum II
hints: Like Combination Sum, but each number can only be used once and there are duplicates.
Sort the array and skip duplicates at the same recursion level.
After choosing a candidate, move to the next index to ensure each element is used at most once.
approach: Sort the candidates. Backtrack by iterating from the current index. Skip duplicates at the same level (if candidates[i] == candidates[i-1] and i > start, skip). Each candidate is used at most once by advancing the index.
key_insight: Sort + skip same-level duplicates (j > i && candidates[j] == candidates[j-1]) eliminates duplicate combinations while allowing each element to be used once.

title: Word Search
hints: Try starting the search from every cell that matches the first letter.
Use DFS/backtracking to explore all four directions from each cell.
Mark cells as visited during exploration and unmark them when backtracking.
approach: For each cell matching word[0], start a DFS. At each step, check bounds, character match, and visited status. Mark the cell visited, recurse in all four directions, then unmark. Return true if the entire word is matched.
time_complexity: O(m * n * 4^L) where L is word length
key_insight: Backtracking on a grid requires marking/unmarking visited cells to prevent revisiting during the current path while allowing other paths to use the same cell.

title: Palindrome Partitioning
hints: Try every possible first partition, check if it's a palindrome, then recurse on the rest.
Use backtracking: at each position, try all substrings starting there that are palindromes.
The base case is when you've partitioned the entire string.
approach: Backtrack from the start of the string. At each position, try all possible substrings ending at different positions. If the substring is a palindrome, add it to the current partition and recurse on the remaining string.
key_insight: At each position, try every possible palindrome substring as the next partition segment — only valid palindromes lead to recursive calls.

title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
hints: Map each digit to its corresponding letters (like a phone keypad).
For each digit, try each of its mapped letters and recurse on the next digit.
Build combinations character by character, one digit at a time.
approach: Create a digit-to-letters mapping. Backtrack through the digits: for each digit, try appending each corresponding letter to the current combination, recurse on the next digit, then backtrack.
time_complexity: O(4^n) where n is number of digits
key_insight: Each digit maps to 3-4 letters, creating a decision tree where each level corresponds to a digit and each branch to a letter choice.

title: N-Queens
hints: Place queens row by row — for each row, try each column and check if it's safe.
Track attacked columns, positive diagonals (r+c), and negative diagonals (r-c) with sets.
If a placement is safe, recurse to the next row; backtrack if no column works.
approach: Place queens row by row. For each row, try each column. Use three sets to track attacked columns, positive diagonals (r+c), and negative diagonals (r-c). If a column is safe, place the queen, update sets, recurse to the next row, then backtrack.
solution_python:
def solveNQueens(self, n: int) -> List[List[str]]:
    res = []
    cols = set()
    posDiag = set()
    negDiag = set()
    board = [['.' ] * n for _ in range(n)]
    def backtrack(r):
        if r == n:
            res.append([''.join(row) for row in board])
            return
        for c in range(n):
            if c in cols or (r + c) in posDiag or (r - c) in negDiag:
                continue
            cols.add(c)
            posDiag.add(r + c)
            negDiag.add(r - c)
            board[r][c] = 'Q'
            backtrack(r + 1)
            cols.remove(c)
            posDiag.remove(r + c)
            negDiag.remove(r - c)
            board[r][c] = '.'
    backtrack(0)
    return res
key_insight: Three sets (columns, positive diagonals r+c, negative diagonals r-c) provide O(1) conflict checking for queen placement.

title: Number of Islands
hints: Each connected group of '1's forms one island.
When you find a '1', use DFS/BFS to mark all connected '1's as visited.
Count how many times you initiate a new DFS/BFS — that's the number of islands.
approach: Iterate through the grid. When a '1' is found, increment the island count and use DFS/BFS to mark all connected '1' cells as visited (change to '0'). Each DFS/BFS call explores one complete island.
key_insight: Each DFS/BFS from an unvisited '1' discovers exactly one island — marking visited cells prevents double counting.

title: Clone Graph
hints: You need to create a copy of every node and replicate all edges.
Use a hash map to track old-node-to-new-node mappings to handle cycles.
DFS/BFS through the graph; for each neighbor, clone it if not yet cloned, then connect.
approach: Use DFS with a hash map mapping original nodes to their clones. For each node, create a clone, then recursively clone all neighbors. The hash map prevents infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
key_insight: A hash map from original to clone node handles cycles — if a node is already cloned, return the existing clone instead of recursing.

title: Max Area of Island
hints: Similar to Number of Islands, but track the size of each island.
DFS/BFS returns the area of the connected component it explores.
Keep track of the maximum area seen across all islands.
approach: Iterate through the grid. For each unvisited '1', run DFS to compute the island's area by counting all connected '1' cells. Track and return the maximum area found.
key_insight: DFS naturally computes island area by summing 1 for the current cell plus the recursive results from all four directions.

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
hints: Instead of checking if each cell can reach both oceans, think in reverse.
Start DFS/BFS from ocean borders — find all cells reachable from each ocean.
Cells reachable from both Pacific and Atlantic borders are the answer.
approach: Run DFS from all Pacific border cells and all Atlantic border cells separately, flowing uphill (to cells with equal or greater height). The intersection of cells reachable from both oceans is the answer.
solution_python:
def pacificAtlantic(self, heights: List[List[int]]) -> List[List[int]]:
    rows, cols = len(heights), len(heights[0])
    pac, atl = set(), set()
    def dfs(r, c, visit, prevHeight):
        if (r < 0 or c < 0 or r >= rows or c >= cols or
            (r, c) in visit or heights[r][c] < prevHeight):
            return
        visit.add((r, c))
        dfs(r+1, c, visit, heights[r][c])
        dfs(r-1, c, visit, heights[r][c])
        dfs(r, c+1, visit, heights[r][c])
        dfs(r, c-1, visit, heights[r][c])
    for c in range(cols):
        dfs(0, c, pac, heights[0][c])
        dfs(rows-1, c, atl, heights[rows-1][c])
    for r in range(rows):
        dfs(r, 0, pac, heights[r][0])
        dfs(r, cols-1, atl, heights[r][cols-1])
    return list(pac & atl)
key_insight: Reverse the problem: instead of flowing water downhill from each cell, flow uphill from ocean borders — the intersection gives cells that reach both oceans.

title: Surrounded Regions
hints: An 'O' is NOT captured if it's connected to the border.
Start from border 'O's and mark all connected 'O's as safe.
After marking, any remaining 'O' is surrounded and should be flipped to 'X'.
approach: DFS/BFS from all border 'O' cells, marking them as safe (e.g., 'T'). Then iterate through the board: convert remaining 'O's to 'X' (they're surrounded), and convert 'T's back to 'O' (they're safe).
key_insight: Reverse thinking: instead of finding surrounded regions, find unsurrounded ones (connected to border) and mark them safe first.

title: Rotting Oranges
hints: All rotten oranges spread simultaneously — this is multi-source BFS.
Start BFS from all initially rotten oranges at the same time.
Each BFS level represents one minute of rotting.
approach: Add all initially rotten oranges to a queue. BFS level by level — each level represents one minute. For each rotten orange, rot its fresh neighbors. Count minutes until no fresh oranges remain. If any fresh orange is unreachable, return -1.
key_insight: Multi-source BFS naturally models simultaneous spreading — all rotten oranges are starting points, and each BFS level is one time step.

title: Walls and Gates
hints: Instead of BFS from each empty room to find the nearest gate, think in reverse.
Start BFS from all gates simultaneously (multi-source BFS).
Each BFS level increments the distance by 1, naturally filling rooms with shortest distances.
approach: Multi-source BFS from all gates. Add all gate cells to the queue. BFS outward — each room reached for the first time gets the shortest distance. Walls and already-visited rooms are skipped.
key_insight: Multi-source BFS from all gates simultaneously fills each room with its shortest distance — no need to BFS from each room individually.

title: Course Schedule
hints: Model courses and prerequisites as a directed graph.
If the graph has a cycle, it's impossible to finish all courses.
Use DFS with three states (unvisited, visiting, visited) to detect cycles.
approach: Build an adjacency list from prerequisites. DFS from each course, tracking visiting state to detect cycles. If a cycle is found, return false. Mark courses as visited once all prerequisites are verified.
key_insight: Course scheduling is cycle detection in a directed graph — a cycle means circular dependencies that make completion impossible.

title: Course Schedule II
hints: This extends Course Schedule — now you need the actual ordering.
Use topological sort: add a course to the result after all its prerequisites are processed.
DFS post-order gives a reverse topological ordering.
approach: Build an adjacency list. DFS with cycle detection (three states). After processing all prerequisites of a course, add it to the output. This produces a valid topological order. Return empty if a cycle exists.
key_insight: DFS post-order naturally produces topological order — a course is added to output only after all its prerequisites are fully processed.

title: Redundant Connection
hints: A tree with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges — one extra edge creates a cycle.
Process edges one by one; the edge that creates a cycle is the redundant one.
Union-Find efficiently detects if two nodes are already connected.
approach: Use Union-Find. Process each edge: if both nodes already share the same root (are connected), this edge creates a cycle and is the answer. Otherwise, union the two components.
key_insight: Union-Find detects cycles in real-time: if an edge connects two already-connected nodes, it's the redundant edge creating the cycle.

title: Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
hints: Each connected component is a group of nodes reachable from each other.
Union-Find: start with n components, each union reduces the count by 1.
Alternatively, count the number of DFS/BFS traversals needed to visit all nodes.
approach: Use Union-Find starting with n components. For each edge, union the two nodes — if they were in different components, decrement the component count. Return the final count.
key_insight: Start with n components; each edge that successfully unions two different components reduces the count by exactly one.

title: Graph Valid Tree
hints: A valid tree has exactly n-1 edges and is fully connected (no cycles).
If edges != n-1, it's immediately not a tree.
Use Union-Find to check for cycles: if an edge connects already-connected nodes, there's a cycle.
approach: Check if edges == n-1. Then use Union-Find to verify no cycles: process each edge, and if both endpoints already share a root, a cycle exists. If no cycle and edge count is correct, it's a valid tree.
key_insight: A graph is a valid tree iff it has exactly n-1 edges and no cycles — Union-Find checks both properties efficiently.

title: Word Ladder
hints: Each word is a node; edges connect words that differ by exactly one letter.
BFS finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
Use wildcard patterns (e.g., h*t for hot) to efficiently find neighbors.
approach: BFS from beginWord. Build an adjacency list using wildcard patterns: for each word, replace each character with '*' to create patterns. Words sharing a pattern are neighbors. BFS level by level to find the shortest transformation sequence.
time_complexity: O(m^2 * n) where m=word length, n=word count
key_insight: Wildcard patterns (h*t) create an implicit graph where words differing by one letter share a pattern — BFS on this graph finds the shortest transformation.

title: Climbing Stairs
hints: The number of ways to reach step n depends on the previous steps.
You can reach step n from step n-1 (one step) or step n-2 (two steps).
This is exactly the Fibonacci sequence: dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2].
approach: Bottom-up DP. The number of ways to reach step i is dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Base cases: dp[1]=1, dp[2]=2. Only need two variables since each state depends on just the previous two.
key_insight: This is the Fibonacci sequence in disguise — each step's count is the sum of the two preceding steps because you can arrive via a 1-step or 2-step jump.

title: Min Cost Climbing Stairs
hints: At each step, you choose to come from one step back or two steps back.
dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]).
You can start from step 0 or step 1, so the answer is min of the last two dp values.
approach: Bottom-up DP. For each step i, the minimum cost to reach it is cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). The answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]) since you can reach the top from either of the last two steps.
key_insight: Each step accumulates the minimum cost to reach it; the answer considers both possible final jumps (from last or second-to-last step).

title: House Robber
hints: At each house, you either rob it (add its value to the best from two houses back) or skip it.
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]).
You only need two variables to track the previous two optimal values.
approach: Bottom-up DP with two variables. For each house, compute max(skip this house = prev max, rob this house = two-back max + current value). Update the two tracking variables.
key_insight: The optimal robbery at each house is the better of skipping it (keep previous max) or robbing it (current value + max from two houses back, avoiding adjacency).

title: House Robber II
hints: Houses are arranged in a circle — the first and last are adjacent.
You can't rob both the first and last house.
Run House Robber twice: once excluding the last house, once excluding the first.
approach: The circular constraint means you can't rob both house 0 and house n-1. Run the linear House Robber on nums[:-1] and nums[1:], return the max of both results.
key_insight: Break the circular dependency by running the linear solution twice — once excluding first house, once excluding last — and taking the maximum.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
hints: A palindrome mirrors around its center.
Try expanding outward from each possible center (both odd and even length).
There are 2n-1 possible centers (n single chars + n-1 gaps between chars).
approach: Expand around center. For each index, try both odd-length (single center) and even-length (two-char center) palindromes. Expand outward while characters match. Track the longest found.
key_insight: Every palindrome has a center — expanding outward from each of the 2n-1 possible centers finds all palindromic substrings in O(n^2) time.

title: Palindromic Substrings
hints: Similar to longest palindromic substring, but count all palindromes.
Expand around each center and count each valid expansion as a palindrome.
Remember to check both odd and even length palindromes.
approach: Expand around center for each position. For both odd and even centers, expand while characters match and increment the count for each valid palindrome found.
key_insight: Each successful expansion from a center produces one more palindromic substring — count every expansion step rather than just tracking the longest.

title: Decode Ways
hints: At each position, you can decode one digit or two digits (if valid).
dp[i] = dp[i-1] (if single digit valid) + dp[i-2] (if two digits valid).
'0' can only be decoded as part of '10' or '20' — it can't stand alone.
approach: Bottom-up DP. At each position, if the current digit is valid (1-9), add dp[i-1]. If the two-digit number ending here is valid (10-26), add dp[i-2]. Handle '0' carefully — it can only pair with '1' or '2'.
key_insight: Each position branches into 1-digit or 2-digit decodings (like Fibonacci), but '0' kills the single-digit branch and two-digit values must be 10-26.

title: Coin Change
hints: For each amount, try every coin denomination.
dp[amount] = min(dp[amount - coin] + 1) for each coin.
Initialize dp[0]=0 and all others to infinity (amount+1 works as sentinel).
approach: Bottom-up DP. For each amount from 1 to target, try each coin: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin] + 1). Initialize dp[0]=0. If dp[amount] exceeds amount, return -1 (impossible).
key_insight: Each amount builds on smaller subproblems — using coin c means 1 + the optimal solution for (amount - c).

title: Maximum Product Subarray
hints: Unlike maximum sum, a negative number can flip min to max (and vice versa).
Track both the current maximum AND minimum product at each position.
When encountering a negative number, swap max and min before multiplying.
approach: Track curMax and curMin at each position. For each number, compute new max and min considering: the number itself, number*curMax, number*curMin. Update the global maximum.
solution_python:
def maxProduct(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
    res = max(nums)
    curMin, curMax = 1, 1
    for n in nums:
        vals = (n, n * curMax, n * curMin)
        curMax, curMin = max(vals), min(vals)
        res = max(res, curMax)
    return res
key_insight: Negative numbers can flip min to max — tracking both the running maximum and minimum product handles sign changes correctly.

title: Word Break
hints: dp[i] represents whether s[0:i] can be segmented into dictionary words.
For each position i, check all possible last words ending at i.
dp[i] = true if there exists j where dp[j] is true and s[j:i] is in the dictionary.
approach: Bottom-up DP. dp[i] is true if s[0:i] can be segmented. For each position i, check if any word in the dictionary matches s[j:i] where dp[j] is true. dp[0] = true (empty string).
time_complexity: O(n * m * k) where m=dict size, k=avg word length
key_insight: Each position is segmentable if it matches a dictionary word AND the remaining suffix is also segmentable — classic DP with string matching.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
hints: dp[i] = length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i.
For each i, check all j < i: if nums[j] < nums[i], dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1).
For O(n log n), maintain a tails array and use binary search.
approach: DP approach: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1) for all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]. Each element starts with dp[i]=1. Return the maximum dp value. O(n^2) time.
key_insight: Each element extends the best subsequence ending at any smaller previous element — dp[i] considers all valid predecessors.

title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
hints: If the total sum is odd, partitioning into equal halves is impossible.
The problem reduces to: can we find a subset that sums to total/2?
Use a DP set tracking all achievable sums; for each number, add it to existing sums.
approach: If total sum is odd, return false. Otherwise, find if a subset sums to total/2. Use a set of achievable sums. For each number, create new sums by adding it to each existing sum in the set.
key_insight: Equal partition = finding a subset summing to total/2. This is the 0/1 knapsack problem, solvable with a DP set of achievable sums.

title: Unique Paths
hints: You can only move right or down — each cell is reachable from the cell above or to the left.
The first row and first column all have exactly 1 path (straight line).
approach: Fill a 2D DP grid where dp[r][c] = number of paths to reach (r,c). First row and column are all 1s. Each other cell sums paths from above and left.
key_insight: Only right and down moves means each cell's path count is the sum from above and left — optimizable to a single row of DP.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
hints: Compare characters at each position in both strings.
If characters match, LCS extends by 1 from the diagonal (dp[i-1][j-1]+1).
If they don't match, take the max of skipping one character from either string.
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = LCS length of text1[0:i] and text2[0:j]. If characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
key_insight: Character match extends the diagonal LCS by 1; mismatch takes the best of either skip — this naturally builds the optimal subsequence length.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
hints: At each day, you're in one of two states: holding a stock or not holding.
After selling, you must wait one day (cooldown) before buying again.
Model as a state machine with states: buy, sell, cooldown.
approach: Use DP with states. At each day: if buying, profit = max(keep holding, buy today from cooldown state). If selling, profit = max(stay idle, sell today). Track cooldown by using the profit from 2 days ago for buying.
key_insight: The cooldown constraint means after selling at day i, the next buy can't happen until day i+2 — model this with a state machine DP.

title: Coin Change II
hints: Count the number of combinations (not permutations) that sum to the amount.
Process coins one at a time to avoid counting different orderings of the same coins.
dp[a] += dp[a - coin] for each coin, processing coins in the outer loop.
approach: Unbounded knapsack DP. Process each coin type in the outer loop (to avoid duplicate orderings). For each amount, dp[a] += dp[a - coin]. dp[0] = 1 (one way to make amount 0).
key_insight: Processing coins in the outer loop ensures each combination is counted once (not permutations) — coin ordering is fixed.

title: Target Sum
hints: Each number is either added or subtracted — think of it as partitioning into two groups.
Use DP where the state is (index, current sum).
Alternatively, this reduces to a subset sum problem: find subsets summing to (total+target)/2.
approach: Use memoized DFS with state (index, current_sum). At each index, branch into adding or subtracting the current number. Count paths that reach the target at the end.
key_insight: Each number creates a binary branch (+/-), but many paths reach the same sum — DP collapses identical states to avoid exponential computation.

title: Interleaving String
hints: At each position in s3, the character must come from either s1 or s2 (in order).
Use 2D DP where dp[i][j] = can s1[0:i] and s2[0:j] interleave to form s3[0:i+j]?
Check if the current s3 character matches the next unused character from s1 or s2.
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] indicates whether s1[0:i] and s2[0:j] can form s3[0:i+j]. Transition: if s1[i-1]==s3[i+j-1], check dp[i-1][j]; if s2[j-1]==s3[i+j-1], check dp[i][j-1].
key_insight: Two pointers (i into s1, j into s2) define the state — at position i+j in s3, we can take from s1[i] or s2[j] if they match.

title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
hints: From each cell, DFS to all four neighbors with strictly greater values.
The increasing constraint means there are no cycles — perfect for memoization.
dp[r][c] = 1 + max(dp of all valid increasing neighbors).
approach: DFS with memoization from every cell. For each cell, explore all four neighbors with strictly greater values. Cache results since the strictly increasing constraint guarantees no cycles.
key_insight: Strictly increasing values guarantee a DAG (no cycles), making memoization safe — each cell's result is computed exactly once.

title: Distinct Subsequences
hints: dp[i][j] = number of ways to form t[0:j] from s[0:i].
If s[i] == t[j], we can either use this match or skip s[i].
If s[i] != t[j], we must skip s[i]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j].
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = number of distinct subsequences of s[0:i] that equal t[0:j]. If s[i]==t[j], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + dp[i-1][j]. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j].
key_insight: When characters match, you have two choices (use the match + skip it), combining both counts. When they don't match, you must skip the s character.

title: Edit Distance
hints: dp[i][j] = min operations to convert word1[0:i] to word2[0:j].
If characters match, no operation needed: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1].
If they differ, try insert (dp[i][j-1]+1), delete (dp[i-1][j]+1), or replace (dp[i-1][j-1]+1).
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = min edits to convert word1[0:i] to word2[0:j]. If chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise, 1 + min(insert, delete, replace) from adjacent states.
key_insight: Three operations map to three adjacent DP cells: insert=right, delete=down, replace=diagonal — take the minimum plus one operation cost.

title: Burst Balloons
hints: Think about which balloon to burst LAST in a range, not first.
If balloon k is burst last in range [i,j], its value is nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1].
dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting all balloons between i and j.
approach: Interval DP. For range [l,r], try each balloon i as the LAST to burst. Its coins = nums[l-1]*nums[i]*nums[r+1] (boundaries remain). dp[l][r] = max over all i of (dp[l][i-1] + coins + dp[i+1][r]).
key_insight: Thinking about which balloon to burst LAST (not first) in a range makes the subproblems independent — the boundaries are fixed.

title: Regular Expression Matching
hints: '.' matches any single character. '*' means zero or more of the preceding element.
dp[i][j] = does s[0:i] match p[0:j]?
For '*', consider two cases: use it zero times (skip pattern pair) or use it once more (if current char matches).
approach: 2D DP where dp[i][j] = whether s[0:i] matches p[0:j]. For '*': either skip the pattern pair (zero matches: dp[i][j-2]) or match one more character if current matches. For '.': matches any character.
key_insight: The '*' operator has two DP transitions: skip the pattern pair entirely (zero matches) or consume one more character from s (if it matches the preceding pattern element).

title: Maximum Subarray
hints: At each position, decide: extend the current subarray or start a new one?
If the running sum becomes negative, it's better to start fresh.
Track the current sum and the maximum sum seen so far.
approach: Kadane's algorithm. Iterate through the array, maintaining a running sum. If the running sum goes negative, reset to 0. Track the maximum subarray sum encountered.
key_insight: A negative running sum only hurts future subarrays — reset to zero and start fresh, since any extension would be worse than starting over.

title: Jump Game
hints: Track the farthest index you can reach as you iterate.
If you ever reach an index beyond your current farthest, you're stuck.
If the farthest reach includes the last index, return true.
approach: Greedy: track the farthest reachable index. Iterate through the array — at each position, update the farthest reach. If the current index exceeds the farthest, return false. If farthest >= last index, return true.
key_insight: Work backwards: if a position can reach the current goal, it becomes the new goal. If the goal reaches index 0, the end is reachable.

title: Jump Game II
hints: Think of this as BFS where each 'level' is one jump.
Track the current jump's farthest reach and the overall farthest.
When you reach the end of the current level, increment jumps and start the next level.
approach: BFS-like greedy. Track the end of the current jump range and the farthest reachable. When you reach the current range end, take a jump (increment count) and extend to the farthest reachable.
solution_python:
def jump(self, nums: List[int]) -> int:
    jumps = 0
    l = r = 0
    while r < len(nums) - 1:
        farthest = 0
        for i in range(l, r + 1):
            farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])
        l = r + 1
        r = farthest
        jumps += 1
    return jumps
key_insight: Each jump explores a 'level' of reachable indices (like BFS) — the minimum jumps equals the number of BFS levels to reach the end.

title: Gas Station
hints: If total gas >= total cost, a solution must exist.
If you run out of gas at station j starting from i, no station between i and j works either.
Reset the starting point to j+1 whenever the tank goes negative.
approach: If total gas < total cost, return -1. Otherwise, greedily find the start: iterate, tracking the tank. If tank goes negative, reset start to the next station and reset tank. The final start position is the answer.
key_insight: If you fail at station j from start i, all stations between i and j also fail — so jump the start to j+1, combining multiple failure proofs in one pass.

title: Hand of Straights
hints: Each group must be W consecutive cards — start each group from the smallest available card.
Sort the cards and greedily form groups starting from the smallest unused card.
Use a frequency map to track remaining cards.
approach: Sort the cards. For each smallest available card, try to form a group of W consecutive cards by decrementing their counts. If any card in the sequence is unavailable, return false.
key_insight: Greedily start each group from the smallest available card — this forces a unique grouping and quickly reveals if valid groups are impossible.

title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
hints: A triplet is usable only if none of its values exceed the corresponding target values.
Filter out triplets where any value exceeds the target.
From remaining triplets, check if each target value appears in at least one triplet.
approach: Filter triplets: discard any where a[i] > target[i] for any position (merging would exceed target). From remaining valid triplets, check if the max in each position can reach each target value.
key_insight: Triplets with any value exceeding the target are unusable (merge only takes max). Among valid triplets, we just need each target position covered by at least one.

title: Partition Labels
hints: Each character must appear in exactly one partition.
For each character, find its last occurrence — the partition must extend at least that far.
Expand the partition end as you encounter characters with later last occurrences.
approach: Record the last occurrence of each character. Iterate through the string, expanding the current partition's end to include the last occurrence of each character. When the current index equals the partition end, close the partition.
key_insight: A partition must contain all occurrences of each character in it — tracking last occurrences and expanding greedily finds minimum partitions.

title: Valid Parenthesis String
hints: '*' can be '(', ')' or empty — track a range of possible open counts.
Maintain leftMin (min possible open parens) and leftMax (max possible open parens).
If leftMax goes negative, too many ')'. If leftMin goes negative, clamp to 0.
approach: Track the range of possible open parenthesis counts [leftMin, leftMax]. '(' increments both, ')' decrements both, '*' decrements leftMin and increments leftMax. Clamp leftMin to 0. Valid if leftMin reaches 0.
key_insight: Instead of trying all '*' possibilities, track the range [min, max] of possible open counts — if 0 is within range at the end, some valid assignment exists.

title: Insert Interval
hints: Split intervals into three groups: before, overlapping with, and after the new interval.
